data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 230: $23,000 Allocated, $2,428.39 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
Just when things seem like they’re going to take a breather, the market just keeps running. Earnings season is in full swing, and big tech set the tone as Alphabet (GOOGL) decimated the bear case with its Q2 earnings. The S&P 500 rallied 1.65% this week while the Nasdaq climbed 0.88% and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio accomplished 2 new milestones. It’s funny how everyone forgets that we were in the middle of a trade war and the market was in bearish territory back in April as the S&P 500 is now up almost 9% for the year. Next week will be very interesting as I am expecting Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META) to all beat the consensus estimates for earnings. If Apple (AAPL) can pull a rabbit out of the hat and show that they’re taking AI seriously by deploying a significant amount of capital to improve their capabilities it could rally as well. The craziest part about where we are today is that S&P 7,000 is less than 10% away. Between a strong earnings season where corporate earnings expand and if the Fed pivots on monetary policy in September my predictions from the beginning of the year may come true. I think that many of the investments I made to gain additional exposure to technology, REITs, and financials will work out very well throughout the second half of 2025. While I don’t expect the Fed to cut rates at the July meeting next week, I do expect them to pivot in September as there is too much corporate debt coming due that was borrowed at significantly lower prices. In the meantime I plan on continuing to allocate capital toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio and building out the forward income stream.

I feel like this was a repeat of last week as the market rallied and three new records were established. After allocating $23,000 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 230 at $30,089.77. The account's profitability increased by 5.15% ($347.07) to $7,089.77, which is a return on invested capital of 30.83%. This week, I started a new position in the Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO) and used the remaining capital to add another share to AGNC Investment Corp. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $43.55 of dividend income in week 230. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $16.45 (0.68%) to $2,429.39. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,473.06 of income since inception, which is 19.53% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The forward dividend income is expanding, and as more capital is generated and reinvested back into the portfolio, I believe the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will experience a large S-curve in the next several years. I am almost to the point where half of the annualized contributions are being generated from the dividend income, and sooner rather than later, they will be exceeded by the dividend income. I believe the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is structured well for the future, and I am excited to have a bit more time to allocate capital toward certain areas before the Fed pivots and starts cutting rates.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I’m very pleased with the performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio as two new milestones were reached this week. The profitability in the account reached $7,000 for the first time. This was the first milestone as the account’s profitability is $7,089.77, which is a 30.83% ROI. The next milestone was that the account balance finished the week at $30,089.77, and $30,000 was exceeded for the first time. I am happy with the results since inception as this account is now producing $2,428.39 in dividend income which is a portfolio yield of 8.07% and a yield on cost of 10.56%. It’s been a long journey, and the account now has over 100 positions generating recurring income. I built the portfolio to mitigate downside risk while generating recurring income. Capital appreciation is a secondary focus, but it’s doing quite well. I have restricted my individual investments to 5% and each sector to 20% of the portfolio. Based on the track record this strategy has achieved my investment goals for this account, and I believe it is positioned well for the future.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $804.52 (33.13%)
  • Equities $573.25 (23.61%)
  • CEFs $406.52 (16.74%)
  • REITs $395.08 (16.27%)
  • BDCs $249.02 (10.25%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 30 weeks in 2025, $1,293.68 of dividend income was produced, which is 79.70% of the total income from 2024. There were 426 positions that paid a dividend, which is 60.08% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $43.55 of dividend income generated this week, which kept the amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis at $43.12 in 2025. This is an increase of $11.91, or 38.15% YoY, and there have been 3 individual weeks when $100 of dividend income was exceeded. I am continuing to add capital toward positions that generate income on a monthly basis which should allow me to generate more weeks that exceed $100 in the future. In the first half of 2025 I generated $1,097.48 of dividend income, and I believe that there is a strong chance I can exceed $1,200 of dividend income being produced in the 2nd half of 2025.

The amount of income generated in July after the $43.55 of dividend income produced last week was $163.18. There are another 4 trading days in July, and there should be a bunch of positions generating their monthly dividends over this period. When I look at the 1st half of 2025 I am a bit surprised that I only exceeded $200 of dividend income once. I think that July is gearing up to be the next $200 month of dividend income, and I am excited to see how Q3 plays out. Based on the trends, I wouldn’t be surprised if I generate one month that falls between $225 - $250 of dividend income in 2025. As I continue to allocate capital the dividend snowball will continuously increase on a monthly basis.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 44 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. These positions are expected to add roughly $371.74 of forward dividend income from those shares. I have tried increasing my position in positions that are lagging, but it’s becoming increasingly difficult. I am still focused on getting another 5-10 positions to cross over by the end of the year, which would get me to roughly 50% of the portfolio.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are trending just above 19% and REITs have fallen below 19%, which I am happy about. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 38.88% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 23.61% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.12% of the portfolio and generate 76.39% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) is back up and represents 4.41% of the portfolio. I can’t seem to get MO down even though I haven’t added to this position since February of 2024. I am very happy that only 3 positions exceed 3% of the portfolio. It doesn’t look like much changed in the top-10 holdings except for where the NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQ) is positioned. I have a feeling that Realty Income (O) is going to start climbing as we get closer to a rate cut making it harder to get below the 3% level. While I have a 5% cap, I would like the portfolio to become less top heavy. As time progresses, the larger positions should represent less of the portfolio, which I believe will help mitigate my risk further.

The top-10 positions didn’t change this week, but QQQI did generate its distributions this week. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,519.06, and they finished the week with a value of $8,773.20. This is a ROI of $2,254.13 (34.58%). There has been $1,172.81 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 17.99% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $628.38 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.64%. These positions represent 29.16% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.88% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 230 Additions

This week I started a new position in Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF (QDVO) and used the remaining capital to add another share to AGNC Investment Corp (AGNC).

Amplify CWP Growth & Income ETF

  • I recently wrote an article on QDVO and discussed my entire investment thesis (can be read here)
  • When I had conducted my research QDVO had generated a 20.52% total return since inception which was less than 1-year ago
  • QDVO tactically writes covered calls, allowing for upside participation while generating recurring income, unlike more rigid covered call strategies, and has outperformed QQQI and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ)
  • Since inception QDVO has generated $2.04 of income with an average monthly distribution of $0.20 which is an 8.16% yield on cost with another 2 distributions on the way to close out the year.
  • I think that QDVO can become one of the most attractive income-focused ETFs in my portfolio as Amplify has done a great job building out this strategy

AGNC Investment Corp

  • I had around $15 left with this week’s allocation and decided to pick up another share of AGNC
  • AGNC has paid a steady monthly dividend of $0.12 and continues to yield over 14% on an annualized basis
  • I think that AGNC is positioned well for the future, and their originations will go through the roof when the Fed cuts rates as the housing market will become unlocked.
  • AGNC is generating such a large dividend that I am able to compound it into several shares each year based on the current position size which increases my dividend snowball organically

Week 231 Game plan

I may be starting another new position this week with the NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI).

Conclusion

It was a great week for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio as the account balance exceeded $30,000, the profitability exceeded 30% as my return on invested capital is $7,089.77 and it's generating $2,428.39 in forward dividend income. This portfolio has turned into a mini-ETF with 103 positions that have a 10.56% yield on cost. I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned for anything the market throws at it, and the S-curve in its forward annualized dividend income is just getting started. As I continue to allocate capital toward this portfolio and reinvest all the income being generated, I think the rate of compounding is going to surprise many long-term readers as new milestones should be accomplished quicker than before. Please leave me all your suggestions in the comment section as I look forward to interacting with everyone.

data.title: Taiwan's GDP Blowout Prompts An Aggressive Growth Forecast Upgrade
data.description:
By Lynn Song

Second-quarter GDP blows expectations out of the water on surge from external demand

Taiwan's second-quarter GDP growth surged to 7.96% year-on-year, up from 5.5% YoY in the first quarter and reaching the highest level since the second quarter of 2021. The quarter-on-quarter growth of 3.1% was also the fastest growth of any quarter since the third quarter of 2020, good for a seasonally adjusted annualised rate of 12.9%. Both the recent highs for YoY and QoQ growth were heavily skewed by base effects from the pandemic, while no obvious boost from base effects impacted this year's second-quarter reading.

Through the first half of the year, Taiwan's GDP has grown 6.7% YoY, more than double the current consensus forecast.

Given the strength of Taiwan's recent trade data, it was unsurprising to see that the primary driver of growth in the second quarter was net external demand, which contributed 5.8ppt. While we had expected the robust trade data to lead to a beat in the second quarter numbers, the magnitude was significantly higher than expected. The outperformance can be attributed to the generally strong demand for AI-related products, but also likely to another wave of front-loading of exports to the US ahead of the 1 August tariff deadline.

The contribution from domestic demand, on the other hand, slowed, with a contribution of 2.2ppt compared to 4.7ppt in the first quarter of the year. The soft consumption data was reflected in a meagre 0.3ppt contribution from private final consumption, but the 1.6ppt contribution from gross capital formation remained relatively solid.

2Q25 GDP surged to nearly 8% YoY

Upgrading Taiwan's 2025 outlook on strong first half and prospect of a trade deal

Overall, Taiwan's economy has fared far better than many market participants had expected at the start of the year. The heavy demand driven by the AI boom, combined with the fear of incoming tariffs, led to blistering export growth year to date.

A 51.3% YoY growth of exports to the US in the first half of the year, punctuated by a whopping 90.9% YoY growth in June, proved to be a major driver of overall export growth. While Taiwan's export growth to other regions has been impressive as well, much of the growth can be attributed to the surge in US demand this year.

As such, this backdrop means that the imminent US tariff decision could play a significant role in Taiwan's outlook moving forward. Securing carve-outs for tech exports and/or a lower overall tariff rate in line with Japan and Korea would be favourable outcomes, while a higher tariff could prove particularly damaging for the growth outlook. Given the strategic importance of Taiwan's exports, as well as the previously announced major investments into the US, our baseline scenario is closer to the former. As of the time of writing, markets have been speculating on still-unconfirmed reports that Taiwan will ultimately receive 15% tariffs in exchange for heavy investment into the US.

Even in a more positive tariff outcome where Taiwan's exports are not disadvantaged compared to its key competitors, a drop-off from export front-loading could lead to a smaller contribution from net external demand in the second half of the year. While growth is still likely to far outpace expectations from a few months back, maintaining the torrid growth of the second quarter will be challenging.

Nonetheless, with data continuing to surprise on the upside, it's worth reconsidering the 2025 outlook. At the start of the month, our 2025 Taiwan GDP forecast was already near the top end of the market consensus. A wave of upward forecast revisions for Taiwan's outlook has occurred as data repeatedly beat forecasts – but the risks to GDP forecasts still look heavily skewed to the upside after the second quarter's GDP surprise and the recent cooling of inflation, especially if a deal is secured to keep tariffs on Taiwanese exports to the US the same as its key competitors. As a result, we are making a more aggressive upgrade to our full-year forecast, raising it from 3.7% YoY to 5.8% YoY.

A cooling of inflation and strengthening of the Taiwanese dollar have created conditions that would allow for a potential rate cut, but with growth wildly beating expectations year-to-date, this has certainly not been necessary yet and looks increasingly unlikely barring an unexpected deterioration of the outlook. We push back our rate cut expectations and now expect the Central Bank of the Republic of China to stand pat in 2025.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: NovoCure: Cash Burn, A Costly Launch, And A Maturing Business Signal More Pain Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Upon doomscrolling social media, I saw that many investors were scratching their heads after NovoCure Limited (NASDAQ:NVCR) reported its second-quarter 2025 earnings, prompting its stock to fall to 52-week lows.

Although the report featured topline beats, there were a few nuggets in it that reinforced my previous bearish take.

My last analysis on NovoCure (I have been following since 2017) was in late February, following its fourth-quarter earnings report. The company warned that its legacy glioblastoma, or GBM, business was “maturing” and was expected to grow at a “low mid-single-digit rate” in 2025. Meanwhile, although it had received approval in other indications, such as mesothelioma and lung cancer, I noted that launching these products would be costly and would have a challenging time achieving clinical adoption. I concluded that future growth in GBM alongside a costly NSCLC launch “signals a challenging year ahead for NovoCure’s stock.” NVCR has since declined 42%.

Recent Developments

Again, topline numbers look good, but that’s hardly what the stock market looks for when a company reports its earnings. NovoCure achieved $158.8 million (up 6% year-over-year) in revenue, topping the analyst consensus estimate by a few million. The company also beat on EPS (GAAP) by $0.02, coming in at -$0.36. However, upon a closer look, there are a few financial issues to note.

For starters, NovoCure’s net loss widened from $33.4 million in Q2 2024 to $40.1 million. This was predominantly the result of two variables. For starters, NovoCure’s gross margin dropped 300 basis points to 74%. The company attributed this to the rollout of the new HFE transducer arrays for use with Optune Gio for GBM. It’s an improvement that makes Optune Gio less cumbersome. Moreover, its NSCLC launch impacted margins as well. Bear in mind that NovoCure has not yet achieved private or public reimbursement for Optune Lua, which was FDA-approved last October. It is common that reimbursement follows approval. So, the company opted to absorb the upfront therapy costs before broad reimbursement is established.

Reimbursement for Optune Lua is a topic in itself. For private payers, TTFields will likely need to be included in the updated National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) guidelines, which are highly influential for coverage decisions and begin in Fall 2025. Public reimbursement is a more formal process. NovoCure has submitted for a Local Coverage Determination (LCD) reconsideration with Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACs). However, if LCD is achieved, clinical adoption will likely hang on NCCN guidelines. So, it is something to watch closely.

Recall that Optune Gio secured an NCCN recommendation (Category 1) years ago for newly diagnosed GBM. It has since been commercially successful, generating around $600 million per annum.

In addition to the newly minted NSCLC indication, Optune Lua is approved for mesothelioma. Despite its approval years ago, “Optune Lua” is absent from the most recent NCCN guidelines. NovoCure recognized a measly $1.3 million from mesothelioma in Q2.

Zooming in on Optune Lua’s prospects in NSCLC, its approval followed the Phase 3 LUNAR trial, in which TTFields therapy proved superior to standard therapy alone. However, despite its success, the relevance is in question. As I’ve discussed in the past, there were several issues with LUNAR. The benefit was observed in only a subset of patients who received TTFields with an immune checkpoint inhibitor, like KEYTRUDA, in second-line. However, as prominent treatment recommendation guidelines note, such as UpToDate, in modern practice, immunotherapy is included in first-line treatment. Moreover, TTFields is cumbersome, requiring the patient to wear the device for 18 hours a day. Subsequently, it is unlikely that Optune Lua will catch “fire” in NSCLC. I also do not believe the NCCN will recommend it.

This is partly why NovoCure’s launch in NSCLC is risky. This leads me to NovoCure’s financials. I ended June 30 with $911.5 million in cash and short-term investments. However, the company owes $559.79 in “convertible notes” that are due in November 2025. The company is prepared to pay this off with existing cash. Moreover, it plans to draw another $100 million from its credit facility. While NovoCure is not in immediate financial distress, it does increase the urgency to secure markets outside GBM. Net cash utilized by operating activities was nearly $16 million in Q2, implying a monthly cash burn of roughly $5.3 million.

Recall that NovoCure released Phase 3 PANOVA-3 trial data in pancreatic cancer last November. However, I pointed out some challenges, such as a potentially marginal survival benefit (is 2.04 months of more life worth the additional cost and burden of incorporating TTFields?), alongside uncertain reimbursement and clinical adoption. To be fair, PANOVA-3 also reported improvements in secondary endpoints like quality of life and "median pain-free survival," which could add to TTField's value proposition. I still don't believe this will inspire significant clinical adoption.

NovoCure is also filing the paperwork to secure approval in NSCLC with brain metastases following METIS Phase 3 trial data. However, as I noted in March 2024, while METIS narrowly hit its primary endpoint in progression-free survival (PFS), it missed on Overall Survival (OS). Other secondary endpoints, such as time to neurocognitive failure and radiological response rate, also missed statistical significance. The trial also included a crossover provision that may have confounded data. Lastly, as always with TTFields, especially in the absence of convincing clinical data, its compliance requirements are burdensome.

All of this serves as a reminder that clinical trial “success” does not always translate to market success. This leads me to my last point. NovoCure’s GBM business is stalling, and launches outside GBM are slow.

Prescriptions for Optune Gio for GBM declined 1% in Q2 year-over-year. Active mesothelioma patients in the last three quarters: 29, 44, and 43. Active NSCLC patients in the last three quarters: 20, 62, and 94. Given that there are nearly 100,000 metastatic NSCLC diagnoses each year in the U.S., Optune Lua is only receiving very niche interest.

Conclusion

To wrap up, recent developments continue to support my bearish stance on NVCR (maintain “Sell”). Although NovoCure’s $1 billion enterprise value appears compelling when viewed alongside ~$600 million in annual revenue, its lack of growth and cash generation merit a discount. Moreover, the combination of cash burn, a reduction in cash buffer, and a costly NSCLC launch (that may prove unsuccessful) has me more cautious.

On the other hand, should NovoCure secure reimbursement in NSCLC and regulatory approvals in pancreatic cancer and brain metastases, its stock could see some upside.

However, to me, it has become apparent that TTFields’ lowest hanging fruit, by a considerable distance, was GBM. Notably, after years of success in this market, there isn’t much else to hang a hat on outside of risky launches in other indications following mixed, and, arguably, irrelevant, clinical data.

data.title: Blackbaud: Growth Drivers Will Be Tough To Sustain As Renewal Cohorts Shrink
data.description:
As the Q2 earnings season continues, we're rather unsurprised to see several large-cap behemoths that have exposure to consumer spending, like Chipotle (CMG) and Starbucks (SBUX) report disappointing sales growth. But one pocket of the market that has surprised us so far are small-cap tech stocks, which are bucking the broader macroeconomy and posting surprisingly resilient results.

Blackbaud (BLKB) is one of these names. The enterprise software company, which specializes in providing tools for the nonprofit and higher education sectors, shot up more than 10% after reporting a beat-and-raise in Q2. The post-earnings rally has nearly erased all of Blackbaud's year-to-date losses, but the critical question for investors is: will Blackbaud continue to rally and attain a more "normal" valuation, or is this just a dead cat bounce?

I last wrote a bearish note on Blackbaud in May, when the stock was trading at more modest levels in the low/mid $60s. Needless to say, I did not anticipate Blackbaud's beat-and-raise in Q2 (especially because macroeconomic headwinds also tend to reduce people's charitable giving, which reduces Blackbaud's transactional revenue streams). That being said, I also remain skeptical that the current growth drivers that Blackbaud is enjoying are truly sustainable, and I remain at a sell rating here.

Valuation Checkup Against Boosted Guidance

First, let's discuss the biggest topic on investors' minds: Blackbaud's guidance increase, and how its valuation looks against that raised guidance amid the post-earnings rally.

As shown in the chart above, Blackbaud boosted its revenue guidance to $1.125 billion at the midpoint, about a 40 bps increase relative to $1.12 billion previously and representing 5% of organic revenue growth (excluding the recent sale of EVERFI). It also boosted its adjusted EBITDA margin by 40 bps to 35.8%, and pro forma EPS by 3% to a new outlook of $4.40.

Meanwhile, at current share prices near $71, Blackbaud trades at a market cap of $3.47 billion. After we net off the $41.6 million of cash (this excludes Blackbaud's more sizable ~$0.9 billion restricted cash balances, as most of this is held on behalf of and due to its customers) and $1.16 billion of debt, the company's resulting enterprise value is $4.59 billion.

Against Blackbaud's new guidance midpoints, the stock trades at:

  • 4.1x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 11.4x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 16.3x FY25 P/E

At face value, Blackbaud is a beacon of value in an otherwise expensive stock market: especially against much more expensive software peers. Yet the core question here is: how sustainable is the company's current pace of mid/high single-digit organic revenue growth?

Q2 Download

Let's now turn the discussion to Blackbaud's latest Q2 results, and the core factors driving Blackbaud's recent growth kick. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Blackbaud's revenue declined -2% y/y to $281.4 million on an as-reported basis, beating Wall Street's expectations of $276.5 million (-4% y/y) by a two-point margin. However, the more important metric here is the company's organic growth rate, since the company sold off its underperforming subsidiary EVERFI earlier this year, which was generating roughly $19 million in quarterly revenue.

The chart below illustrates that after excluding EVERFI from prior-year results, Blackbaud's organic revenue growth in Q2 would have clocked in at 6.8% y/y, which is 100 bps stronger than 5.8% y/y in Q1.

To cut to the chase: a ~16x P/E for a mature software company that is truly growing organic revenue at a ~7% pace would be a bargain in today's market. Companies like Adobe (ADBE) and Salesforce (CRM), which are showcasing similar high single-digit revenue growth, are trading closer to ~20x earnings.

And yet, the weakness that Blackbaud is masking is the fact that, at least to me, much of its revenue growth is temporary once it laps its price increases. As a reminder for investors: Blackbaud in mid-2023 instituted a new renewal pricing scheme, wherein customers renewing to a new three-year contract get hit with a mid/high teens increase in Year 1, followed by single-digit price increases in Years 2 and 3 (and beyond).

Where I acknowledge Blackbaud has been successful is in retaining customers, despite these heavy price increases. However, as seen in the chart above: the company's largest renewal cohorts were in 2023 and 2024. Only a smaller ~25% slice of Blackbaud's customer base is eligible for the Year 1 renewals this year, while an even smaller 10% slice is up for renewal next year.

This means that the portion of the customer base that is subject to the sharp mid/high teens price increase upfront is shrinking (while the prior-year compares become tougher, because a larger portion of the customer base is reset on higher prices). To me, this headwind will become even more pronounced next year, when ~90% of Blackbaud's customer base is already on the new higher contract terms.

Of course, the upside scenario here is if Blackbaud restarts the cycle again and hits customers with a second mid/high teens price increase in Year 4 and beyond - but I doubt the company can do this without triggering significant pushback or churn.

In the near term, we note that price increases are helping Blackbaud to boost its profitability. Pro forma operating margins in Q2 hit 33.5%, a 350bps y/y improvement over 30.0% in the year-ago Q1. Still, I expect these benefits to be transitory as Blackbaud's renewal cohort shrinks in 2026 - making it difficult for me to recommend a buy here.

Key Takeaways

While I acknowledge that Blackbaud's organic revenue growth performance has been robust in a tough economy (clearly the company is achieving high levels of customer retention), I'm concerned about Blackbaud's choppy transactional revenue, its heavy debt loads, and tougher prior-year compares once we head into 2026 and Blackbaud has already reset the vast majority of its customer base onto higher contract pricing. To me, I'd prefer to take advantage of Blackbaud's near-term price spike and lock in gains rather than risk a downward multiples correction once organic growth rates decelerate again.

data.title: Neurocrine Biosciences Positioned For Growth And New Potential Blockbusters
data.description:
Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX) is a biopharmaceutical company that develops treatments for neurology and neuroendocrinology. I believe they have a strong commercial portfolio and an expanding pipeline. Their two most important commercial drugs are Ingrezza and Crenessity (launched in Q1 2025). Still, I think it's worth reviewing their latest Phase 3 trial, as well as Crenessity's early commercial success. Also, Ingrezza has continued to show it can hold a respectable market share. And since there are multiple readouts expected later this year, I continue to believe NBIX is a solid “Buy” at these levels.

Still Mostly An Ingrezza Play

Neurocrine Biosciences is a biopharmaceutical company that develops treatments for neurological, neuroendocrine, and neuropsychiatric conditions. NBIX was founded back in 1992 and is currently headquartered in San Diego, California. I previously covered NBIX, where I focused on their promising fundamentals and Crenessity's potential following its FDA approval. Since then, the stock has fluctuated, and it's roughly down 5.0%. Nevertheless, I continue to see potential with NBIX for longer-term investors.

You see, their portfolio includes four FDA-approved drugs that were discovered and developed in-house. However, the two most important are undoubtedly Ingrezza (valbenazine) and Crenessity (crinecerfont). The two other in-house drugs are Orilissa (elagolix) and Oriahnn (elagolix + estradiol/norethindrone). Orilissa is mostly for severe pain associated with endometriosis therapy, while Oriahnn helps with heavy menstrual bleeding due to uterine fibroids.

Similarly, NBIX wholly owns its IP and North American rights for Alkindi, a replacement therapy for adrenal insufficiency in pediatric patients. The same goes for Efmody's rights, which is a drug intended for congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH). However, it's worth mentioning that NBIX doesn't commercialize these two last-mentioned products directly. Instead, NBIX sells them through a deal with Eton Pharmaceuticals (ETON).

Ingrezza's dose is administered once daily via oral administration, with no complex titration required. It is compatible with mental health regimens, which is important since many Huntington's disease (HD) patients also receive antipsychotics or antidepressants. And note that Ingrezza is approved for tardive dyskinesia (TD) and HD chorea. Both disorders involve involuntary movements, but TD is drug-induced, and HD is genetic and systemic.

As for Crenessity, this drug is approved for classic CAH, and it was recently launched. NBIX believes it can be a potential future blockbuster due to its large target audience. Management estimates its potential peak sales at roughly $1 billion, and so far, we've seen strong early figures. In Q2 2025, Crenessity generated $53 million, totaling $67.5 million in 1H2025. I mostly agree with management's optimism on Crenessity since it addresses a major unmet need. You see, Crenessity allows for hormone control with lower steroid doses, all while having no major side effects.

2025 Pipeline Progress And Protected Pricing

Moreover, NBIX's pipeline is progressing nicely, as they've recently announced two Phase 3 initiations for 2025. First, NBI‑568 is an M4 agonist for schizophrenia that started a registrational study in April 2025. This was after its Phase 2 data demonstrated symptom improvement with once-daily dosing. The second Phase 3 is for Osavampator in Major Depressive Disorder [MDD] for patients who are inadequate responders to standard antidepressants. In my view, Osavampator in particular could soon become a new revenue vertical if they succeed with their MDD Phase 3.

Likewise, their R&D pipeline on muscarinic receptor programs includes NBI‑'570, which is a dual M1/M4 agonist. However, this is in earlier stages in Phase 1 for schizophrenia, which will proceed to Phase 2 later in 2025. Also, NBIX expects to start a Phase 2 trial of NBI-568 in bipolar mania later this year. Two more compounds, NBI-567 (M1 agonist) and NBI-569 (M4 agonist), are also in Phase 1 and are poised to provide readouts in 2025. Additionally, on June 30, 2025, a Phase 1 started for NBIP-1435, indicated for CAH. And note, this marks a milestone as it's NBIX's first biologic peptide candidate.

However, not those two compounds were initially licensed from Nxera (OTCPK:SOLTF) and hold promise for the treatment of neuropsychiatric disorders. But overall, I wouldn't yet consider them major value drivers since we still have to wait for more concrete results at this stage. Fortunately, we also know that Ingrezza qualified for pricing protection under the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA). This was due to the “Small Biotech Exception” that exempts certain drugs from Medicare price negotiations.

In my view, this tax designation delays potential government price-setting pressures on Ingrezza, which I believe should help NBIX preserve its favorable margins. In fact, Ingrezza showed more patient-reported outcome data from a real-world study called KINECT-PRO. There, patients reported benefits from taking Ingrezza, which reflect improvements in general well-being. So, all in all, I believe Ingrezza is resonating with patients, which is great news since this is NBIX's main income source. And since NBIX is now targeting a new drug every 2 years, its longer-term prospects also appear promising.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, NBIX trades at a sizeable $13.3 billion market cap. Their latest Q2 report shows their balance sheet holds $1.9 billion in cash, equivalents, and marketable securities as of June 30, 2025. Those are liquid resources they can use to fund their R&D and operations. They also don't have any financial debt, other than regular operating liabilities. And their latest book value reached $2.7 billion, indicating a somewhat pricey P/B of 4.9. For comparison, their sector's median P/B is just 2.4, so there's clearly a bit of premium embedded in the shares at these levels.

However, Ingrezza is capable of treating both conditions, which is largely why it's been NBIX's main blockbuster drug. Clearly, that's why NBIX remains mostly a bet on Ingrezza at these levels, since it's largely its main revenue vertical. For context, Ingrezza alone produced $624.4 million in sales, and NBIX's total Q2 revenues were $687.5 million. So, that implies Ingrezza generated 90.8% of their quarterly revenues. Of course, this means Ingrezza dominance is still high, but I imagine it should decrease over time as Crenessity's revenues grow.

Having said that, NBIX's 2025 sales guidance now projects approximately $2.50 to $2.55 billion. That would price NBIX at a more reasonable P/S of roughly 5.3, which is still significantly more expensive than its peers' median forward P/S of 3.6. Naturally, I suppose management's guidance assumes a relatively quick ramp-up for Crenessity's sales figures in the coming couple of years.

However, if such growth doesn't materialize quickly, it should be discounted in its valuation multiples. Nevertheless, Seeking Alpha's dashboard projects NBIX will generate $3.1 billion by 2026. So, that means by 2026, NBIX would add another $1.1 billion in revenues YoY (using the lower end of its 2025 guidance). That implies a substantial YoY growth of 24.0%, which shows NBIX remains a high-growth biotech in its sector overall. But for now, I think it's fair to say NBIX trades at a premium overall.

I also believe NBIX will likely continue growing its IP portfolio going forward with label expansions and new IPs. For example, we know they're working on Ingrezza's new indications for dyskinetic cerebral palsy and adjunctive schizophrenia. Unfortunately, their recent Q2 press release noted they missed their primary endpoint for schizophrenia in its Phase 3 trial. So, clinical trial risks like these remain a factor worth considering, which is partly why I refrain from giving NBIX a “Strong Buy” for now.

Fortunately, Crenessity's broader adoption in CAH could increase its TAM as well. And Osavampator also initiated its Phase 3 trial for MDD, which is another sizeable TAM estimated at roughly $10.2 billion by 2034. So, NBIX has optionality that still justifies its current price tag in my view. Still, there's a final risk to consider with NBIX. The reality is that its valuation doesn't leave much room for upside in the near term.

And this means any setbacks on its core revenue vertical (Ingrezza) or delays on new IP additions could easily send the stock substantially lower. This headline risk implies that, ideally, you should wait for a pullback on NBIX before building a sizeable position.

Likewise, I estimate NBIX Q2 generated a positive cash flow again this quarter, as its EBIT rose to $145.6 million. Similarly, its net income reached $107.5 million in Q2. So, I think it's fair to say they're likely self-sustainable at this point, which largely de-risks NBIX's investment thesis as well. That's why, despite the valuation concerns I previously mentioned, I feel there's enough value and potential at these levels to warrant a “Buy” rating on NBIX nonetheless.

Conclusion: Reasonable “Buy”

Overall, I believe NBIX has continued making good progress on its two main IPs. Those two main revenue pillars should sustain NBIX's positive cash flows, essentially making it a self-sustainable biotech, which largely derisks its investment thesis. Likewise, I like the added optionality of new label expansions on Ingrezza and Crenessity, while attempting to bring a new drug to market every two years. In my view, this is a recipe that should accrue significant shareholder value over time, which is why I think NBIX is a solid long-term bet.

data.title: Sell Alert: 3 REITs That Will Likely Cut Their Dividend
data.description:
Over the past year, I have predicted many dividend cuts in the REIT sector (VNQ).

As an example, earlier this year, I predicted that Global Medical REIT (GMRE) would soon cut its dividend, and it just slashed it by 29%.

Similarly, last year, I predicted that Crown Castle (CCI) would likely need to reduce its dividend, and it did. In a separate article, I also predicted that Easterly Government Properties (DEA) and Service Properties Trust (SVC) would cut, and they did as well.

Today, I am back with another round of dividend cut predictions.

I think that the following REITs are unlikely to sustain their dividends, unless we get lucky and enjoy very large rate cuts in the near term.

Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN)

Brandywine is a very popular REIT amongst high-yield seeking investors because it owns high-quality assets, and yet, it offers a 14% dividend yield that's fully covered by its FFO. Moreover, the REIT already cut its dividend once in 2023, which has led many to believe that the current dividend rate should be sustainable since it was already reduced:

I hate to be the bearer of bad news, but the 20% cut of 2023 likely wasn't enough, and another dividend cut is now likely just around the corner.

The REIT owns mostly office properties, most of which are located in Philadelphia, which is struggling today.

These are high-quality assets and should perform reasonably well over the long run due to the "flight to quality", but they are still facing significant headwinds given how tough the office market has become. Vacancy rates are at an all-time high of about 20%, resulting in significant competition to secure new tenants. It is forcing all landlords, including those of superior properties, to offer significant leasing incentives and tenant improvement packages. Therefore, Brandywine's FFO metric is not a good measure of its actual cash flow, as it is likely to face significant capex over the coming years.

Adjusted for that, its payout ratio is not sustainable at all.

Yes, the dividend is covered by FFO, but the FFO ignores the capex.

The payout ratio based on actual cash available for distribution is estimated to be between 120-150% this year, according to the management's own guidance:

And things will not magically get much better in 2026.

On the contrary, I think that the coming labor market disruption caused by the AI revolution could very negatively affect near-term leasing. Companies don't know how many white-collar workers they will need in the future, and so it is reckless to sign long-term leases right now.

Making things even worse, Brandywine has quite a lot of leverage. Its debt-to-EBTIDA is around 7x, and the REIT has many development projects underway, which adds further uncertainty to the story.

I predict a 30-50% dividend cut in the near future.

Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR)

I am less certain about this prediction, but I think that Innovative Industrial Properties is likely to cut as well.

The cannabis REIT has an amazing track record and a strong balance sheet with just a 15% LTV, but it has faced growing tenant issues over the past year, with its biggest tenant, representing 17% of its cash flow, defaulting on its lease.

It has since then found a resolution to this particular tenant, but other defaults have occurred since then, and more are likely coming.

Cannabis operators are having a very hard time in this market, with lots of them losing money and needing to raise capital to fund operations. But after a multi-year bear market in this sector, raising capital has become a lot harder and highly dilutive.

Despite not fully covering its dividend, IIPR has defied the odds so far and maintained its dividend, hoping that conditions would soon improve, allowing it to preserve its dividend track record.

But given that the market is not giving it any credit for its dividend anyway, pricing it at a 14% dividend yield, they would be prudent to cut it now to retain more cash flow and be in a stronger position to negotiate with tenants as future lease defaults occur.

Its peer, NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP), intentionally aims to retain at least 10-20% of its cash flow to be in a position of strength when these inevitable setbacks occur.

Community Healthcare Trust (CHCT)

Community Healthcare Trust is another popular REIT that's today offering a seemingly sustainable 11% dividend yield.

It is the highest-yielding REIT in the healthcare property right now, and it is often pitched as a "recession-resistant 11% yielder" given that recessions are unlikely to significantly impact the performance of its skilled nursing facilities.

But here's the issue.

The REIT is facing tenant defaults, which have depressed its cash flow. They have been mostly buying high cap rate assets with yields up to 10%. You don't get such high cap rates without taking significant risks.

Moreover, it has quite a bit of debt and relatively short debt maturities, which will likely lead to rising interest expense.

Even then, its current payout ratio based on FFO is already slightly above 100%. Adjusted for capex and leasing incentives, it is closer to 110-120% by our estimates.

This likely won't end well for shareholders.

I have seen this play out many times in the past, and the dividend is rarely sustained in the end when a REIT is already overpaying and dealing with many headwinds simultaneously.

I would consider buying a position if it cut its dividend and its share price crashed to much lower levels. I will keep monitoring it closely.

Closing Note

Not all REIT dividends are safe.

Most REITs are well-positioned to grow their dividends as the sector leverage is historically low, payout ratios are conservative, and rents keep on growing.

But there are exceptions that likely won't be able to sustain their dividends, and for this reason, you need to be very selective when investing in this sector.

At High Yield Landlord, we invest in only one out of ten REITs analyzed on average.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Teradyne Just Flipped The AI Switch: Breakout Earnings, Volume, And A Possible Re-Rating In Progress
data.description:
Just went through Teradyne’s (NASDAQ:TER) Q2 2025 numbers and figured I’d share a breakdown for anyone tracking the name. Not a blockbuster quarter, but definitely some interesting signals under the hood, especially if you’re following the AI test space.

Revenue came in at $652M, down about 4.7% YoY. Not great, but mostly expected. The memory test business is still soft (cyclical dip + digestion of HBM4 capacity from last year), and robotics also showed weakness. That said, AI-related SoC demand showed up in a big way and helped to partially offset the whole thing.

Gross margin took a hit, down 170 bps to 57.3%. The pressure mostly came from lower volumes in robotics, not surprising given that segment’s ongoing struggles. On the bright side, Semi Test margins stayed strong thanks to higher ASPs and product mix from AI workloads.

On the cost side, OpEx continues to rise - but it’s deliberate. G&A is estimated at $47M (up ~7%), which lines up with inflation and some cost structure expansion. R&D jumped ~11% YoY, now around $177M. Management already said they’re leaning in here to support AI, HBM, and robotics initiatives, so this increase isn’t a surprise. Sales & Marketing was mostly flat, with a slight increase (~2%), probably helped by the robotics consolidation they mentioned last quarter.

Operating margin dropped to 15.1% (non-GAAP), down 250 bps from a year ago. Not ideal, but again, they’re in investment mode, and the revenue mix (more robotics + less memory) dragged things down a bit. Profit margin (GAAP) was ~11.4%, versus 13.0% last year, with the added headwind of a higher tax rate (13.5% vs. ~10%).

Free cash flow was $132M, down 6.4% YoY but still respectable given the margin compression. Working capital efficiency helped here. EBITDA came in around $98.5M, a ~17.9% drop, but they’re still putting up decent profitability in what’s arguably a transitional quarter.

The balance sheet’s still clean. Cash & equivalents fell to $339M from $553M, largely due to heavy capital return activity - $117M in buybacks and $19M in dividends this quarter alone, totaling $316M returned YTD. That’s 138% of FCF, which shows how aggressive they’ve been in rewarding shareholders. Zero debt on the books, which gives them flexibility as AI-related programs ramp and memory starts to recover.

Revenue Growth Drivers

Now I want to share some thoughts after digging into what’s really driving Teradyne’s business right now, especially coming out of Q2 2025. The numbers were fine, but the real story is in the setup for the second half and beyond. If you’re following the AI hardware and test ecosystem, there’s a lot to like here - especially around mix shift, margin setup, and longer-term optionality.

AI compute and networking are becoming core drivers. Let’s start with the obvious: AI compute is no longer a “nice-to-have” growth angle for Teradyne, it’s becoming foundational. Compute-related revenue made up about 20% of the SoC segment this quarter, so north of $80M, and they’re guiding for significant growth in Q3 and Q4. You’ve got major hyperscalers and merchant GPU/AI ASIC customers ramping test volumes, and the company is clearly seeing an inflection coming in the back half.

Networking demand is also outperforming. It’s flying under the radar, but a big chunk of that is tied to buildouts of AI data center infrastructure. Every test socket and interconnect matters when these hyperscalers are rolling out next-gen AI clusters. CEO Greg Smith even said directly: “The majority of Semi Test revenue in the second half will be AI-driven.” That’s a strong directional signal.

Memory was the weakest spot in Q2 (only $61M), but that could change quickly. What’s happening is that HBM4 capacity buildouts are finally starting to see test demand - specifically, post-stack testing is picking up due to yield losses in final AI accelerator packaging. That could create incremental volume and expand TAM over the next few quarters.

Still a drag in 2025, but there’s a clear pivot happening here. Teradyne has a “plan-of-record” win with a large industrial customer that starts contributing in 2026, and that could be a big needle-mover for Universal Robots. They’re refocusing robotics on scalable, high-volume industrial applications, and they’ve already consolidated sales and service to get leaner. Add in the new U.S. manufacturing facility (for redundancy and customer proximity), and you’ve got the pieces for real structural improvement - just not this year.

Now, On to Margins - This Is Where Things Get Interesting

AI mix is lifting gross margin. AI SoC testing isn’t just growing - it’s more profitable. Higher ASPs and better test throughput make it a margin enhancer. SoC is now over 60% of Semi Test revenue and skewing increasingly toward AI workloads. Meanwhile, memory and robotics are still lower-margin, so the mix shift is doing a lot of the heavy lifting on margins right now.

Volume leverage is starting to kick in. The guidance for Q3 shows this clearly. Gross margins are projected to rise to 56.5-57.5%, with operating margins pushing back toward ~19.5%. That’s all volume-based leverage - better absorption of fixed costs, especially in Semi Test engineering and infrastructure. Basically, they’re getting paid for the capacity they already invested in.

Robotics margin expansion is a 2026 story. The cool part here is that UR and MiR are vertically integrated. That means once volumes ramp, the gross margin scale can be meaningful. Combine that with the cost-cutting they’ve done this year - especially around platform consolidation and unified sales/service - and they’re setting up for margin improvement when revenue returns. Just not in 2025.

SG&A is under control. Despite all the investment in AI, photonics, and robotics R&D, overall OpEx was flat YoY at $275M in Q2. They’re clearly keeping a tight lid on SG&A. R&D is where the spending is focused, and that’s strategic - it’s going into high-return areas, not bloated admin layers.

So yeah, not a blowout quarter, but the AI momentum is real, and they’re clearly investing for what they see coming in H2 and 2026. If you’re long, it’s all about watching how quickly AI ramps offset the lag in robotics and memory.

Price Action And Key Points

Alright, turning to the price action around the stock, Teradyne just went full beast mode today - stock ripped +18.88% and closed right near the high of the day. Absolute monster candle. No upper wick, all buying, straight-up bullish marubozu-type move. What makes it even more interesting is that this didn’t come out of nowhere - the chart had been coiling for weeks in a tight range around $85-95, so today’s breakout looks like a legit momentum ignition, kicked off by earnings. You can check my full chart breakdown here, it has a play button where you can keep tabs on the idea progress.

What’s wild is how clean the technicals are lining up. MACD flipped bullish from below zero a few sessions ago and is now widening - a classic signal that momentum’s turning up. RSI is pushing into overbought territory (around 73), but it’s not divergent, so nothing to panic about. And the volume? Easily one of the biggest days in six months. Feels like the market finally woke up to this name. This doesn’t scream FOMO; it feels more like a sentiment shift or even a re-rating in real-time.

If you like trading breakouts, this is a textbook move. TER had been stuck between $85 and $95, and today it blasted through that range with force. A new box is forming somewhere around $95-107, and if you use the measured move logic (add the height of the box to the breakout), you get a potential price target of $117. That lines up pretty well with the Fib extensions too - it already tagged the 100% extension intraday at $110.50, and the next upside levels look like $124.86 (127.2% Fib) and possibly $140+ if things really get cooking.

Smart money vibes are all over this. The base from April to July looked like slow accumulation - not much action, low volume - and then today boom: gap up, no fade, straight move to the highs with volume behind it. No upper wick means no real profit-taking yet. If this was distribution, you’d see some selling into strength. You’re not.

Bottom line

This is a clean breakout with serious momentum behind it, likely the earnings were the trigger. If you missed the move, keep an eye on any pullback into the $102-104 zone. Target-wise, $117-125 is reasonable in the short term if no reversal shows up, but also remember to keep a tight stop if you see a reversal candle or big-volume rejection. Honestly, this is one of those rare setups where technicals, volume, and macro (AI test tailwinds post-earnings) all come together. Worth watching closely.
